mappings.rationaleAI: Benjamin's The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction, with its concepts of aura and authenticity in mass culture, directly addresses theories concerning the nature, functions, and effects of art and the impact of mechanical reproduction on artistic works.

The Muses' role in Greek mythology as presiders over the arts, the concept of divine inspiration versus human craft, and their influence on Renaissance and Romantic conceptions of creativity directly address the creative process and performers as interpreters/creators.

The Frankfurt School's critique of the culture industry (Adorno), analysis of mass culture, and synthesis of Marxist and Freudian approaches to art directly address theories concerning the functions and effects of art.

Aristotle's Poetics establishes mimesis (imitation) as the foundational theory of art, examines tragedy's functions (catharsis), and validates poetry's philosophical purpose -- directly addressing diverse theories of art's nature and functions.

Photography's invention raises fundamental questions about art versus mechanical reproduction, the status of photography as art, and the democratisation of image-making -- addressing theories of art and distinctions among art kinds.

Taste as a philosophical concept covering debates about subjective versus universal standards (Addison, Burke, Hume, Kant) and the relationship between aesthetic taste and social class directly addresses theories of art and style in the arts.
The changing status and social role of the artist from medieval craftsman to Renaissance genius to modern celebrity addresses diverse conceptions of art and period styles.

20th-century architecture's competing movements (Art Nouveau, Bauhaus, Brutalism, Postmodernism) and the tension between useful and fine art directly address classification of arts by useful/fine distinction and period styles.

Sturm und Drang's rebellion against Enlightenment rationalism and celebration of emotion, individual genius, and nature addresses expressive theories of art and period style as a precursor to Romanticism.

Romanticism's emphasis on emotion, imagination, and individual genius, its reaction against Neoclassicism, and its enduring impact on concepts of creativity directly address expressive theories of art, the creative process, and period style.
The Bauhaus's integration of fine art with crafts and industrial design and its pedagogical innovations directly address the distinction between useful and fine art and 20th-century period style.
